subject: ap-chemistry
title: Unknown Metal Iodide: Molar Mass & Electrochemistry
stimulus:
To determine the molar mass of an unknown metal, M, a student reacts iodine with an excess of the metal to
form the water-soluble compound MI$_2$, as represented by the equation M + I$_2$ → MI$_2$. The reaction proceeds until all
of the I$_2$ is consumed. The MI$_2$(aq) solution is quantitatively collected and heated to remove the water, and the
product is dried and weighed to constant mass.
Data: Mass of beaker = 125.457 g; Mass of beaker + metal M = 126.549 g; Mass of beaker + metal M + I$_2$ = 127.570 g; Mass of MI$_2$ (first and second weighings) = 1.284 g

parts.prompt: Given that the metal M is in excess, calculate the number of moles of I$_2$ that reacted.

scoring_points.description: For correct moles of I$_2$ with proper mass determination: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using 127.570 as the mass of I$_2$ — must subtract the mass before I$_2$ was added.
common_traps: Mass of I$_2$ = (beaker + metal + I$_2$) − (beaker + metal) = 127.570 − 126.549.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the number of moles.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly subtracts the (beaker + metal) mass to isolate the I$_2$ mass added, then converts to moles using the molar mass of I$_2$ (253.80 g/mol).
Uses the total (beaker + metal + I$_2$) mass as the mass of I$_2$ itself; must subtract the (beaker + metal) mass first to get the mass of I$_2$ added.

parts.prompt: Calculate the molar mass of the unknown metal M.

scoring_points.description: For correct molar mass calculation (~65 g/mol): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Since M is in excess and I$_2$ is limiting: $n$(MI$_2$) = $n$(I$_2$) = 4.023 × 10$^{-3}$ mol
Mass of M in MI$_2$ = 1.284 g − (mass of I contribution)
Mass I in MI$_2$ = 2 × 126.9 × 4.023 × 10$^{-3}$ = 1.021 g
Mass M = 1.284 − 1.021 = 0.263 g
Molar mass M = 0.263 g / 4.023 × 10$^{-3}$ mol = 65.4 g/mol (zinc)

wrong_examples: Not subtracting the I mass — must find mass of M alone in MI$_2$.
Using the wrong stoichiometry — from M + I$_2$ → MI$_2$, 1 mol M reacts with 1 mol I$_2$.
common_traps: Molar mass of M = mass of M / moles of M. Mass of M = mass of MI$_2$ − mass of I$_2$ used (since M:I$_2$ = 1:1).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the number of grams of M and the molar mass.

sample_responses.response: Moles M = moles I$_2$ = $0.004023\text{ mol}$. Mass M = $1.284\text{ g MI}_2 - 1.021\text{ g I}_2 = 0.263\text{ g M}$. Molar mass = $\frac{0.263\text{ g}}{0.004023\text{ mol}} = 65.4\text{ g/mol}$
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the 1:1 stoichiometry (M + I$_2$ → MI$_2$) to set moles of M equal to moles of I$_2$, subtracts the I$_2$ mass from the MI$_2$ mass to isolate the mass of M, and divides to obtain the molar mass — matching the CB model answer of 65.4 g/mol (zinc).

sample_responses.response: Molar mass M = $\frac{1.284\text{ g}}{0.004023\text{ mol}} = 319\text{ g/mol}$
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the mass of MI$_2$ (1.284 g) instead of the mass of M alone (0.263 g) — must subtract the iodine mass to get the mass of M.

parts.prompt:
Propose an experimental test the student could perform that could be used to support the hypothesis that
the compound formed is ionic. Explain how the results of the test would support the hypothesis if the
substance was ionic.
parts.scoring_notes: Dissolve in water and test conductivity, OR melt the compound and test conductivity. If ionic, mobile ions carry charge → solution/melt conducts electricity. Alternatively, test high melting point or hardness. Most commonly accepted: conductivity of aqueous solution.

scoring_points.description: For a valid experimental test with correct ionic outcome prediction: 1 point

required_elements: Dissolve in water and test electrical conductivity (or test molten sample).
If ionic, the solution/melt will conduct electricity because ions are mobile charge carriers.
alternatives.correct_example: Dissolve a sample of MI$_2$ in water and connect the solution to a conductivity apparatus. If the compound is ionic, the dissolved ions (M$^{2+}$ and I$^-$) will carry electrical charge, causing the circuit to conduct electricity and the light bulb (or meter) to respond. A covalent compound would not dissociate into ions and the solution would not conduct.

wrong_examples: Checking if the compound dissolves in water — ionic compounds are often soluble, but covalent compounds can be too; conductivity is more diagnostic.
common_traps: The key test for ionic vs. covalent is conductivity: ionic → dissociates into ions → conducts. Covalent → does not produce free ions → does not conduct.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for an appropriate test AND for explaining how the results would support the hypothesis.

sample_responses.response: The student could dissolve the compound in water (or melt it) and test whether the solution/melt conducts electricity. If the solution/melt conducts, mobile ions capable of carrying charge must be present, so the compound is likely ionic.
sample_responses.commentary: States a valid diagnostic experiment (conductivity of solution or melt) and correctly links the observable outcome (conduction) to the presence of mobile ions — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: The student could heat the compound. If it has a very high melting point, the compound is likely ionic.
sample_responses.commentary: Alternative CB-accepted test — ionic compounds typically have very high melting/boiling points due to strong Coulombic attractions throughout the lattice.

sample_responses.response: The student could check whether the compound dissolves in water. If it does, it is ionic.
sample_responses.commentary: Solubility in water is not diagnostic — many covalent compounds (sugar, ethanol) also dissolve in water; conductivity or melting point is the correct diagnostic.

parts.prompt:
Explain why I$_2$ is a solid at room temperature whereas Br$_2$ is a liquid. Your explanation should clearly
reference the types and relative strengths of the intermolecular forces present in each substance.
parts.scoring_notes: Both I$_2$ and Br$_2$ are nonpolar and only have London dispersion forces (LDF). I$_2$ has more electrons and a larger, more polarizable electron cloud than Br$_2$. Larger electron cloud → stronger LDFs → stronger intermolecular forces → I$_2$ is a solid at room temperature while Br$_2$ is only a liquid.

scoring_points.description: For LDF reasoning with I$_2$ having stronger LDFs due to more electrons: 1 point

required_elements: Both I$_2$ and Br$_2$ are nonpolar → only London dispersion forces.
I$_2$ is larger with more electrons and a more polarizable electron cloud.
Stronger LDFs in I$_2$ → higher melting/boiling point → solid at room temperature.
alternatives.correct_example: Both I$_2$ and Br$_2$ are nonpolar molecules; the only intermolecular forces are London dispersion forces. I$_2$ has more electrons (106 vs. 70) and a larger, more polarizable electron cloud. Larger electron clouds create stronger temporary dipoles and therefore stronger LDFs. The stronger LDFs in I$_2$ require more thermal energy to overcome, giving I$_2$ a higher melting point and making it a solid at room temperature, while the weaker LDFs in Br$_2$ leave it as a liquid.

wrong_examples: Claiming I$_2$ has hydrogen bonds or dipole–dipole forces — both are nonpolar and have only LDFs.
common_traps: LDF strength increases with molecular size (more electrons → larger, more polarizable electron cloud → stronger induced dipoles).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for identifying the forces in each substance as London dispersion forces AND explaining why the forces are stronger in I$_2$ than in Br$_2$.

sample_responses.response: Both Br$_2$ and I$_2$ are nonpolar molecules, so the only intermolecular forces are London dispersion forces. LDFs are stronger in I$_2$ because it is larger with more electrons and a more polarizable electron cloud. Stronger LDFs in I$_2$ result in a higher melting point, so I$_2$ is a solid at room temperature while Br$_2$ is a liquid.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies LDF as the only IMF, attributes the size/polarizability difference to I$_2$'s larger electron cloud, and connects the stronger LDFs to the higher melting point — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: I$_2$ has hydrogen bonds, which are stronger than the dipole–dipole forces in Br$_2$.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect — both I$_2$ and Br$_2$ are nonpolar diatomic molecules with neither hydrogen bonds nor dipole–dipole forces; only London dispersion forces act between molecules of each substance.

parts.prompt:
Which solution should the student add to I$_2$(s) to reduce it to I$^-$(aq)? Justify your answer, including a
calculation of E° for the overall reaction.
$H_2O$(aq)  /  Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$(aq)  /  Na$_2$S$_4$O$_6$(aq)
parts.scoring_notes: The student needs a reducing agent to reduce I$_2$ to I$^-$. The half-reaction I$_2$(s) + 2e$^-$ → 2I$^-$(aq), E° = +0.54 V, must occur as reduction; a reducing agent is oxidized. Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$: S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ is oxidized to S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$, E°(oxidation) = −0.08 V (reversed from +0.08 V for S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$ reduction). $E°_{cell}$ = 0.54 − 0.08 = 0.46 V > 0 (spontaneous). H$_2$O cannot reduce I$_2$ (E° would be negative). Na$_2$S$_4$O$_6$ is the product, not a reductant.

scoring_points.description: For Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ with correct E° calculation: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$(aq). The S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ ion is oxidized to S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$ (E° = +0.08 V for the reverse). $E°_{cell} = E°_{I_2/I^-} - E°_{S_4O_6^{2-}/S_2O_3^{2-}} = 0.54 - 0.08 = +0.46\text{ V}$. Positive $E°_{cell}$ confirms the reaction is spontaneous.

wrong_examples: H$_2$O — water cannot reduce I$_2$ spontaneously under standard conditions.
Na$_2$S$_4$O$_6$ — this is the oxidized product of the reaction, not a reducing agent.
common_traps: The reducing agent is oxidized. Must find the substance with a sufficiently negative reduction potential to give a positive overall E° when paired with I$_2$ reduction.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct choice AND 1 point is earned for a correct justification including the E° calculation.

sample_responses.response: Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$(aq) should be selected. The reaction between S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$(aq) and I$_2$(s) is thermodynamically favorable because $E°_{cell} = 0.54 - 0.08 = +0.46\text{ V}$ (positive), so $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$ is negative.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the thiosulfate solution, shows the E° calculation using the two half-reactions, and connects the positive E° to thermodynamic favorability — the CB model answer.
The O$_2$/H$_2$O couple has $E° = +0.68\text{ V}$, so pairing it would give a negative $E°_{cell}$ ($0.54 - 0.68 = -0.14\text{ V}$); water cannot reduce I$_2$ spontaneously.

parts.prompt: Write the balanced net-ionic equation for the reaction between I$_2$ and the solution you selected in part (e).

scoring_points.description: For the correct reduction half-reaction: 1 point
wrong_examples: Omitting state symbols — (s) for I$_2$ and (aq) for I$^-$ are important.
common_traps: I$_2$ is a solid at room temperature; I$^-$ is the aqueous ion product.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The CB scoring guideline awards 1 point for the correct balanced net-ionic equation overall; this granular breakdown (reduction half-reaction) supports the full overall equation required by the rubric.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct reduction half-reaction with proper state symbols; I$_2$ gains 2 electrons to form 2 iodide ions.
Not balanced — each I$_2$ requires 2 electrons to form 2 I$^-$ ions (charge and mass balance).

scoring_points.description: For the correct oxidation half-reaction: 1 point
wrong_examples: Reversing: S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$ → S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ — that is the reduction, not the oxidation.
common_traps: S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ is the thiosulfate reductant; it is oxidized to tetrathionate S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The CB scoring guideline awards 1 point for the correct balanced net-ionic equation overall; this granular breakdown (oxidation half-reaction) supports the full overall equation required by the rubric.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct oxidation half-reaction — two thiosulfate ions combine and release 2 electrons to form tetrathionate.
This is the reverse (reduction) — S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ is the reductant here and must be oxidized to S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$, not produced from it.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced overall net-ionic equation: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Overall: I$_2$(s) + 2S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$(aq) → 2I$^-$(aq) + S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$(aq)
Both half-reactions involve 2 electrons, so no multiplication is needed.

wrong_examples: Including Na$^+$ — Na$^+$ is a spectator ion and must be excluded from the net-ionic equation.
common_traps: Net-ionic equation excludes spectator Na$^+$ ions. The balanced equation has 2:1 stoichiometry of S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ to I$_2$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct balanced net-ionic equation: I$_2$(s) + 2S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$(aq) → 2I$^-$(aq) + S$_4$O$_6^{2-}$(aq).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct overall balanced net-ionic equation — spectator Na$^+$ ions are excluded, both atoms and charge balance (overall charge −4 on each side), and the 2:1 thiosulfate:iodine stoichiometry reflects the 2-electron transfer.
Includes spectator Na$^+$ ions — a net-ionic equation must show only the species that actually change; sodium remains as an aqueous cation throughout and is omitted.
